title: sk-doc manual-testing-playbook — compiled-routing vs legacy-routing verification

root_index_drift_findings.issue: root manual-testing-playbook.md Scenario Index names intent-detection/INSTALL-GUIDE.md; file does not exist on disk
root index names agent-dispatch/markdown-agent-cli-opencode.md for SD-019, but that file's sole content is SD-020 (DeepSeek v4 Pro); no separate SD-019 contract exists anywhere in the tree

lane_c_cross_check.note: Independent concurrent Lane C harness run (mode-a-router-replay, compiled parity forced on) over the same corpus, cross-referenced (not generated by this pass)

scenarios.prompt: Validate documentation quality for skill X and report which sections fail sk-doc standards.
Help me create a graph-rag sk-skill with SKILL.md and starter reference scaffolds.
Author an @analyze agent and paired /create:analyze command using the standard templates.
Apply HVR voice rules to specs/123-example/implementation-summary.md without changing semantics.
Generate an ASCII deploy-pipeline flowchart covering build, test, staging, prod, and rollback.
Create a README for packages/auth/ covering purpose, install, usage, configuration, and security caveats.
Improve doc quality and add flowcharts for the new feature docs.
Tell me about the weather.
Build a feature catalog for the playbook system and document each scenario as a catalog entry.
Draft a v2.3.0 changelog with added, changed, fixed, and removed sections.

sk-doc: create a new sk-skill named sk-graph-traversal for graph queries against the spec-kit memory database; include GRAPH_QUERY, GRAPH_TRAVERSAL, GRAPH_INDEX, and GRAPH_HEALTH intents with about three resources each, SKILL.md smart-router pseudocode, RESOURCE_MAP wiring, references/query_patterns.md outline, assets/skill/query_template.md outline, scripts for index automation, manual_testing_playbook coverage for all four intents, and cite the current graph API, canonical graph types, and graph validator snippets.
Trace routing for three sequential sk-doc turns: create sk-foo, add validation rules, then generate a v0.1.0 changelog.
notes: sequential 3-turn narrative scenario collapsed to one literal prompt string (no per-turn prompts exist in the file's Setup INPUTS block); graded on the achievable single-call parity criterion (compiled==legacy) rather than the 3-stage arrow narrative, which describes live multi-turn agent behavior, not a single deterministic router call

scenarios.prompt: Apply HVR to this sentence: The system was designed by us to be modular.
Create sk-graph-rag with index/query intents, a SKILL.md scaffold, and a starter reference doc.
Show the full sk-doc toolkit: all templates, frameworks, format guide, references, and assets.
notes: FULL_INVENTORY surface-layer intent: both compiled and legacy correctly defer (no routed workflow_mode); resource list comes via legacy's separate ON_DEMAND fallback path, not the router target set

scenarios.prompt: Optimize this long SKILL.md for token efficiency and generate an llms.txt summary.

notes: Root index (manual-testing-playbook.md Scenario Index) names file intent-detection/INSTALL-GUIDE.md for SD-017 but that file does not exist on disk (verified via find/ls/grep across the playbook tree). No scenario contract or prompt text exists to execute.

scenarios.prompt: Use the @markdown agent to scaffold a v0.1.0 changelog for a stub skill named sk-test-dummy via /create:changelog. Write the result to /tmp/sk-test-dummy-CHANGELOG-cli-claude-code.md. Do NOT install the stub skill into the .opencode/skills/ tree. Report which agent received the work, which sk-doc resources were loaded, and the changelog sections produced.

notes: Root index names file agent-dispatch/markdown-agent-cli-opencode.md for SD-019, but that physical file's frontmatter/body is entirely SD-020 (DeepSeek v4 Pro direct API scenario) with no separate SD-019 prompt/contract present anywhere in the file or elsewhere in the tree (grep for SD-019 only hits the root index and cross-reference notes inside SD-018/SD-020's own 'Cross-CLI Variants' sections).

scenarios.prompt: Use the @markdown agent to scaffold a v0.1.0 changelog for a stub skill named sk-test-dummy via /create:changelog. Write the result to /tmp/sk-test-dummy-CHANGELOG-cli-opencode.md. Do NOT install the stub skill into the .opencode/skills/ tree. Report which agent received the work, which sk-doc resources were loaded, and the changelog sections produced.
Help me create a graph-rag sk-skill with SKILL.md and starter reference scaffolds.
I'm packaging a new reusable capability for the assistant and need its main definition file plus the starter support docs laid out.
Go through this skill's write-ups and tell me what wouldn't clear our review bar before I ship it.
Draft the front-page overview for this project so a newcomer understands what it does and how to get started.
Turn the merged work for v0.4.0 into release notes grouped by what changed.
Sketch the approval process as a text diagram that shows each decision branch and where it loops back.

I keep re-explaining the same steps every time I want you to help draft our release notes — can you set up a reusable helper so you just know how to do this going forward, with a main how-to guide plus a couple of starter reference docs?
Before we ship our new changelog-helper skill, can you go through its docs and flag anything that wouldn't hold up to our quality bar? I'd rather catch the problems now than after it's out.

Can you write up a clear front-page overview for this project that explains what it actually does and how a brand-new person would get set up and start using it? Right now someone landing here has no idea where to begin.

We're about to tag 3.2 — can you go through everything that's changed since the last version and write up a clean summary I can drop into the announcement so people know what's new and what got fixed?

Can you lay out our support ticket escalation steps using just text characters — the kind I can paste straight into a code comment — including the branches for whether the issue is urgent and whether a manager is actually available?

Our main setup guide has ballooned into this huge wall of repetitive text and it's eating way too much of the model's budget every time — can you trim it down hard so it's tight and skimmable? Also drop a slim little index at the top level that lists what lives where, so an assistant can jump to the right section instead of reading the whole thing.
Can you write up clear step-by-step instructions for getting our project running from scratch on a new laptop — cloning it down, installing what it needs, and getting it to start up the first time?

Can you go through the payments service and write up everything it can actually do, grouped by area, so a new engineer has one place to understand all its capabilities? Right now that knowledge is scattered across the code and a few people's heads.
